text.title: Indexing Strategies and Hybrid Search
text.summary: HNSW and IVF parameter trade-offs, quantization options, hybrid search configuration, and production index rules.

section.title: HNSW Parameters

rule.statement: Always use HNSW over IVFFlat in production; IVFFlat requires tuning nprobe for recall-speed trade-off that HNSW manages automatically.
rule.rationale: HNSW provides superior recall/speed balance at production scale. IVFFlat is faster to build but requires careful nprobe tuning that shifts as the dataset grows.

section.p: Key HNSW parameters: M (connections per node, default 16, range 12-48; higher = better recall + more memory), ef_construction (build quality, default 100, range 100-500; higher = better index, slower build), ef_search (query quality, default 40, range 50-200; higher = better recall, slower queries).

rule.statement: For pgvector: always run `VACUUM ANALYZE` after bulk inserts and set `hnsw.ef_search` based on recall target before querying.
rule.rationale: pgvector does not auto-update statistics after bulk inserts. Without `VACUUM ANALYZE`, the query planner uses stale statistics and may skip the vector index entirely.

section.title: Quantization

rule.statement: Enable scalar quantization (INT8) by default in Qdrant production deployments; only use binary quantization when memory cost is critical and 5-10% recall loss is acceptable.
rule.rationale: Scalar quantization provides 4x memory reduction with 1-2% recall loss. Binary quantization provides 32x reduction with 5-10% recall loss — suitable only for cost-critical bulk workloads.

section.p: Quantization options: None (baseline, full accuracy), Scalar INT8 (4x memory reduction, 1-2% recall loss), Product PQ (8-32x, 3-5% loss), Binary (32x, 5-10% loss). Start with scalar + rescoring for production.

rule.statement: Before switching a production collection to binary quantization, run an offline recall evaluation against your query set and require explicit approval.
rule.rationale: Binary quantization's 5-10% recall loss is not uniform — it may disproportionately affect certain query types or topic domains in your dataset.

section.title: Hybrid Search

rule.statement: Start with hybrid search alpha=0.7 (favor vectors over BM25) and tune based on evaluation; do not default to pure vector search for RAG production pipelines.
rule.rationale: Vector search handles semantic similarity but misses exact entity names, acronyms, and rare terms. BM25 handles exact matches. Hybrid consistently outperforms either alone for technical queries.

section.p: Alpha parameter (Weaviate): 0 = pure BM25, 0.5 = balanced, 1 = pure vector. For Qdrant, use native sparse + dense vector hybrid with RRF fusion.

section.title: Production Configuration Rules

list.item: Always create payload indexes on fields used in filters BEFORE bulk upsert, not after
Use batch upserts of 100-500 points; single-point inserts are 10-50x slower at scale
Prefer gRPC over HTTP for Qdrant when inserting or querying at >1,000 QPS
Set score_threshold on queries to avoid injecting low-relevance chunks into LLM context
For multi-tenancy: use Qdrant named collections per tenant for full isolation

section.reference: Qdrant collection creation with HNSW + scalar quantization + payload index
